insights: No statistically significant differences exist in resolution time performance across the five agents, even after controlling for case mix (category, priority, location), confirming that observed variation is not attributable to individual agent capability.

The dataset contains a severe systemic data quality issue: 52.8% of records show negative resolution times, with invalid timestamps escalating from 7.3% in Jan 2023 to 97.1% in Jan 2024—indicating a time-dependent artifact unrelated to agent behavior or operational performance.

All five agents have nearly identical ticket volumes (100 each) and comparable invalid record rates (46–58%), confirming that the timestamp anomaly is uniformly distributed and does not bias agent-level comparisons.

None of the key operational dimensions—category, priority, or assignement_group—explain resolution time variance (all ANOVA p > 0.45), and the OLS model explains only 5.2% of total variance (R² = 0.052), indicating that resolution time is driven by unmeasured or non-structural factors.

The 27.4% raw gap in mean resolution time between Fred Luddy (2984 hrs) and Charlie Whitherspoon (3800 hrs) is not statistically robust (p = 0.0778 for their pairwise comparison), and all agents exhibit similarly high coefficient of variation (58–81%), reflecting consistent instability rather than differentiated performance.

Location is the only dimension showing significant inter-agent variation (p = 0.0081), yet location itself has no measurable effect on resolution time—suggesting that geographic assignment differences do not translate into performance outcomes.
summary: The analysis conclusively refutes the premise that human agent performance discrepancies drive resolution time variation: no agent-level effects remain significant after controlling for case mix, and all operational dimensions fail to explain meaningful variance. The dominant finding is a systemic data integrity failure—timestamp inversion affecting over half the dataset and intensifying sharply over time—which invalidates raw resolution time metrics and confirms that observed gaps reflect data artifacts, not operational reality. Given the absence of evidence for individual underperformance and the lack of explanatory power in workload composition or structural factors, optimization efforts must shift away from agent-level interventions and instead prioritize root-cause investigation of the timestamp generation or migration process, particularly the January 2024 data pipeline break. Next focus should be auditing upstream data capture logic and validating temporal consistency before any further performance benchmarking or process improvement initiatives.
